text: In this study, acquired resistance to PD-1 blockade immunotherapy in patients with melanoma was associated with defects in the pathways involved in interferon-receptor signaling and in antigen presentation. (Funded by the National Institutes of Health and others.)
summary: In patients with melanoma treated with PD-1 blockade immunotherapy, defects in pathways involved in interferon-receptor signaling and in antigen presentation were associated with acquired resistance to the therapy.

text: Durable responses in metastatic cancers have been achieved with a variety of immunotherapies such as interleukin-2, adoptive cell transfer of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes, antibodies that block cytotoxic T-lymphocyte–associated antigen 4 (CTLA4), 1 – 5 and antibodies that block programmed death 1 (PD-1). 6 – 10 However, in a recent study, approximately 25% of patients with melanoma who had had an objective response to PD-1 blockade therapy had disease progression at a median follow-up of 21 months. 11
summary: Approximately 25% of patients with melanoma who had an objective response to PD-1 blockade therapy experienced disease progression at a median follow-up of 21 months.

text: Approximately 75% of objective responses to anti–programmed death 1 (PD-1) therapy in patients with melanoma are durable, lasting for years, but delayed relapses have been noted long after initial objective tumor regression despite continuous therapy. Mechanisms of immune escape in this context are unknown.
summary: In patients with melanoma, anti–programmed death 1 (PD-1) therapy produces objective responses; approximately 75% of these responses are durable and last for years. Mechanisms of immune escape in this context are unknown, and delayed relapses have been noted long after initial objective tumor regression despite continuous therapy.
